nativeRange.habitat: Shallow protected coral reef environments, silty sand and rubble bottoms, lagoons and inner reef slopes typically at depths of 0–10 m.

decorPreferences: fine sand bed at least 5 cm deep for burrowing

diet.notes: Has been documented feeding on benthic invertebrates in the wild, including small crustaceans, molluscs and sea urchins. In captivity, generally accepts vitamin-enriched frozen mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, finely chopped meaty seafood, and quality marine pellets or flakes. Tends to do best with several small feedings per day.

summary: An Indo-Pacific Halichoeres wrasse that develops a pastel green body and intricate reticulate facial markings in male phase. Generally considered one of the more peaceful Halichoeres in community reef aquariums, though commonly noted as reef-safe with caution due to predation on small ornamental invertebrates.
careNotes: Best housed in a well-established saltwater aquarium with abundant live rock for foraging and refuge, open swimming space, and a fine sand bed at least 5 cm deep — the species buries itself in the sand at night and when startled, so a coarse or thin substrate is generally considered unsuitable. A tightly fitting lid is widely recommended, as Halichoeres wrasses have been known to jump from open-top tanks. Generally peaceful toward unrelated tankmates, though older, larger specimens have been reported to show aggression toward smaller, more passive fish (including other wrasses) and toward conspecifics; best kept singly, as a male/female pair, or as a small harem of one male with several females. Considered reef-safe with caution — it does not directly harm corals or clams and has been reported to control flatworms and pyramidellid snails, but cannot distinguish between desirable and undesirable invertebrates and will tend to consume ornamental shrimp, fan worms and other small crustaceans. Like most Halichoeres species, this is a protogynous (female-first) sequential hermaphrodite, with juveniles and females showing dark dots that fade as mature males develop the green base colour and reticulate head pattern. Tank-size recommendations across sources varied widely (LiveAquaria/Aquatics Unlimited 75 US gal / ~284 L vs ReefApp 600 L); given the species' adult size of roughly 18–20 cm, active swimming habit and territorial tendency toward conspecifics, the larger value (rounded to 500 L) was adopted. Reported temperature ranges also varied (LiveAquaria/Aquatics Unlimited ~22–26°C; ReefApp ~26°C; FishBase preferred tropical Western Central Pacific waters) — a blended 22–26°C range was used.
breedingNotes: Breeding in captivity has not been meaningfully documented and is generally considered impractical in home aquaria. In the wild the species pairs during spawning and is a protogynous hermaphrodite.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Original wrasse-reef-safe collection URL did not contain a dedicated Green Wrasse page; swapped to the LiveAquaria species product page found via search. Care level, temperament, reef compatibility, water parameters (temp 72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12), 8" max size, 75 US gal minimum tank, sand-burrowing and tight-lid notes.

additional.name: Reef2Reef wiki
additional.notes: Designated secondary returned HTTP 403; could not be fetched. Swapped to ReefApp and FishBase below for cross-verification.

additional.name: ReefApp
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (19 cm), 600 L recommended minimum tank, jumping risk, protogynous hermaphroditism, sand-bed requirement, native range (Philippines to Great Barrier Reef), reef-safe-with-caution (predates ornamental shrimp/tubeworms/sea urchins, eats flatworms and pyramidellid snails), salinity around SG 1.026. Larger tank value adopted per playbook tank-size rule and rounded to 500 L.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Labridae, Eupercaria/misc), max size (19 cm SL), native range (Western Central Pacific: Philippines to Great Barrier Reef), 0–10 m depth, diet (molluscs, crustaceans, sea urchins), distinct pairing during breeding, sexual dichromatism (males show reticulate head pattern), IUCN Least Concern.

additional.name: Aquatics Unlimited
additional.notes: Cross-checked 75 US gal minimum tank, 8" adult size, water parameters (72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, KH 8–12), reef-safe rating, jumping risk and need for tight-fitting lid, predation on red planaria, fireworms and pyramidellid snails, recommendation to add before larger/more aggressive tankmates.

fish.breedingNotes: Spawning has not been meaningfully achieved in home aquaria. Protogynous hermaphrodite — females may transition to male under social pressure.
